threats.title: Critical Lock Duration Bypass via Flawed Unlock Time Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setLockDuration` function contains a critical math flaw that allows users to reduce their unlock time to a timestamp in the past, entirely bypassing the intended lock duration.
threats.investigation_rationale: This directly violates the core economic invariant of the protocol (time-locked assets). An attacker can lock assets for a long duration to maximize rewards, then immediately bypass the lock to withdraw.
preconditions: User has an existing locked position
Sufficient time has passed since `lastLockTime` such that `block.timestamp - lastLockTime > remaining_duration`

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unlock time validation uses a different base timestamp than the actual assignment
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Anytime after a lock has matured past a certain threshold

operational_questions: Can this be executed atomically in a single transaction?
Does this affect all configured tokens simultaneously?
validation_requirements: Test `setLockDuration` with a duration that satisfies the validation but results in a past `unlockTime`

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Lock a large amount of tokens for a long duration to accrue maximum rewards/NFTs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait until `block.timestamp` is sufficiently greater than `lastLockTime`
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call `setLockDuration` with a carefully calculated `_duration` to pass the check but set `unlockTime` to the past
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call `unlock` to withdraw all tokens immediately

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete drain of TVL by bypassing lock mechanics
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade or pause to stop withdrawals

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic understanding of smart contract math
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Requires waiting a specific amount of time after initial lock

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users withdrawing tokens before their intended lock duration expires
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large deposits followed by rapid duration reduction

threats.title: Permanent Fund Freezing via Zero-Value lockOnBehalf Griefing
threats.hypothesis_description: The `lockOnBehalf` function allows any user to indefinitely extend another user's lock duration by locking a zero or dust amount on their behalf.
threats.investigation_rationale: This allows a malicious actor to permanently freeze the funds of any user at near-zero cost, severely impacting user trust and protocol functionality.
preconditions: Victim has an active locked position

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Third-party can modify the state of a user's lock without their consent

operational_questions: Can this be automated to continuously grief users?
Is there any cost to the attacker for ERC20 tokens?
validation_requirements: Test `lockOnBehalf` with 0 quantity for an ERC20 token

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify users with large locked balances nearing their unlock time
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call `lockOnBehalf` with 0 tokens (or 1 wei of ETH) targeting the victim
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat periodically to permanently freeze funds

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users permanently unable to access their funds
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade to fix the logic and allow withdrawals

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic scripting to monitor and execute transactions
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Execute right before victim's lock expires

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Frequent `lockOnBehalf` calls with 0 or dust amounts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users complaining about extended lock times
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Targeted griefing of specific high-value accounts

threats.title: Protocol Insolvency and Locked Funds with Non-Standard ERC20 Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract uses standard `transfer` and `transferFrom` calls without `SafeERC20`. If a token that does not return a boolean (e.g., USDT) is configured, all transfers will revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: The project documentation explicitly states that 'Weird ERC20 Support' (including missing return values) is expected. The failure to use `SafeERC20` violates this requirement and will permanently lock funds if such a token is configured.
deployment_assumptions: Admin configures a token that does not return a boolean on transfer (e.g., USDT)
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation explicitly requires support for tokens with missing return values.
preconditions: Admin configures a non-standard ERC20 token (e.g., USDT)
User attempts to lock or unlock the token

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Direct use of `transfer` instead of `SafeERC20` despite explicit requirements
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin must configure the specific token

operational_questions: Which specific tokens are planned for deployment?
Will USDT be supported on the target chain?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock USDT token and attempt to lock/unlock
Verify the transaction reverts due to ABI decoding failure

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users lock USDT, transaction may succeed or fail depending on the exact token implementation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users attempt to unlock USDT, transaction reverts permanently
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Funds are permanently stuck in the contract

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of user funds for specific tokens
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade to implement SafeERC20

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users unable to withdraw certain assets

threats.title: Accounting Insolvency from Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract credits users with the exact `_quantity` specified during a lock, without verifying the actual amount received. For fee-on-transfer tokens, this leads to protocol insolvency.
threats.investigation_rationale: The project documentation explicitly requires support for fee-on-transfer tokens. The current implementation will result in the contract holding fewer tokens than it owes to users.
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation explicitly requires support for fee-on-transfer tokens.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Crediting user balance based on input parameter rather than actual received amount
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin must configure the specific token

operational_questions: Are fee-on-transfer tokens planned for immediate deployment?
Is there a mechanism to recover from insolvency?
validation_requirements: Lock tokens and verify the contract balance is less than the user's recorded quantity

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Contract balance is significantly lower than total recorded locked quantities
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: First users to unlock receive their full (inflated) amount, last users receive nothing

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol insolvency, late withdrawers lose all funds
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires admin to inject funds to cover the deficit

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed `unlock` transactions due to insufficient contract balance
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mismatch between total locked quantity and actual contract balance
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Race to withdraw before funds are depleted

threats.title: Permanent Loss of NFT Remainders when Locking Outside Lockdrop
threats.hypothesis_description: If a user locks tokens outside of an active lockdrop period, their previously accumulated `remainder` (progress towards an NFT) is unconditionally overwritten to 0.
threats.investigation_rationale: This causes a direct loss of value for users who interact with the protocol during normal operations, penalizing them for locking tokens outside of promotional periods.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `uint256 remainder;` initialized to 0, then `lockedToken.remainder = remainder;` outside lockdrop block

preconditions: User has a non-zero `remainder` from a previous lockdrop

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State variable is overwritten with an uninitialized local variable

operational_questions: Is it intended behavior to penalize users for locking outside of a lockdrop?
How much value does a remainder represent?
validation_requirements: Lock tokens during a lockdrop to accumulate a remainder
Lock tokens after the lockdrop ends
Verify the remainder is reset to 0

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User participates in a lockdrop and earns a partial NFT remainder
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User locks more tokens to earn standard rewards
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User's previous remainder is permanently deleted

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of user trust and value
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires manual state correction or compensation

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must occur outside of a lockdrop period

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Users losing their remainder balances upon locking
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users avoiding the protocol outside of lockdrops

threats.title: Price Feed Governance Manipulation via Disapprove-then-Approve Voting
threats.hypothesis_description: A price feed operator can call `disapproveUSDPrice` followed by `approveUSDPrice` on the same proposal, incrementing both the approval and disapproval counters.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the mutual exclusivity of voting, allowing a single operator to artificially inflate disapproval counts while still supporting the proposal, potentially causing unexpected governance failures.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `approveUSDPrice` does not check if the user has already disapproved

preconditions: A USD price proposal is active
Caller is a designated Price Feed operator

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Lack of cross-checking between approval and disapproval state mappings

operational_questions: Can this be used to intentionally brick the price feed?
What are the default threshold configurations?
validation_requirements: Have an operator disapprove, then approve

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Both counters are incremented, potentially causing the proposal to fail execution despite having enough approvals
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires one malicious or mistaken operator

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Partial - manipulation of price feed governance
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to update USD prices, leading to stale oracle data
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - requires submitting a new proposal
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: USD price updates and weighted value calculations

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Same address calling both disapprove and approve for the same proposal
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Proposals failing to execute despite reaching the approval threshold

threats.title: Inflexible USD Price Updates Causing Oracle Inaccuracies
threats.hypothesis_description: The `proposeUSDPrice` function accepts an array of token contracts but only a single `_price`. This forces all tokens in the proposal to be updated to the exact same USD price.
threats.investigation_rationale: If operators attempt to update multiple tokens in a single transaction to save gas, they will inadvertently set them all to the same price, causing severe valuation distortions for the protocol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Single `_price` parameter applied to all `_contracts` in a loop

preconditions: Operators submit a proposal containing multiple different token contracts

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Array of targets but single value applied to all
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires price feed operators to submit the proposal

operational_questions: Is the operational procedure to only update one token per proposal?
validation_requirements: Submit a proposal with WETH and USDB
Verify both receive the exact same USD price

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Operators decide to update prices for WETH and USDB
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: To save time/gas, they include both in a single proposal with the price of WETH ($3000)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: USDB is now valued at $3000, allowing USDB depositors to claim massively inflated rewards

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires submitting new proposals to correct the prices

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Tokens with drastically incorrect USD prices
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users exploiting mispriced tokens to farm rewards

threats.title: Gas Exhaustion DoS in Core Functions due to Unbounded Token Array
threats.hypothesis_description: The `configuredTokenContracts` array grows unboundedly with each new token configuration. Functions like `setLockDuration` and `getLockedWeightedValue` iterate over this entire array, leading to a permanent DoS if the array becomes too large.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the admin configures many tokens over the protocol's lifecycle (even if they are later marked inactive), core functionalities will eventually exceed the block gas limit and break permanently.
deployment_assumptions: Admin configures a large number of tokens over time
threats.config_evidence: Deployment scripts show multiple tokens being configured, but no mechanism exists to remove them from the array.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `for (uint256 i; i < configuredTokensLength; i++)` iterating over unbounded array

preconditions: Admin has configured a large number of tokens (e.g., > 100)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unbounded iteration over a state array that only grows
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Once the array reaches a critical size

operational_questions: How many tokens are expected to be supported?
Is there a plan to deprecate and remove old tokens?
validation_requirements: Attempt to call `setLockDuration` and verify it reverts due to out-of-gas

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin routinely adds new tokens for various promotional events or lockdrops
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The gas cost of iterating the array exceeds the block limit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users can no longer change their lock duration, and external contracts can no longer calculate weighted values

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent breakage of core protocol features
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade as there is no function to remove tokens from the array
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Lock duration updates and reward distribution
